key_insight: Modularity only helps continual learning when the model operates in a lower-dimensional, richer representational regime; in high-dimensional lazy regimes, modular and monolithic networks perform similarly.
engineering_relevance: For adaptive engineering systems, the result suggests that robustness and transfer depend more on how representational subspaces are allocated than on simply enforcing hard module separation.
method: Compared a task-partitioned recurrent network against a single-network baseline.
Studied sequential A-B-A continual learning with tasks of varying similarity.
Induced high- and low-dimensional regimes by changing weight initialization scale.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that in the high-dimensional regime both architectures show similar performance and geometry, while in the lower-dimensional regime the modular network learns graded task-specific subspaces that better organize similar and dissimilar tasks.
watch_item: The benefit of modularity appears regime-dependent, so results may not generalize if initialization scale or representational dimensionality differs from the tested setting.
paper_title: Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Helps in Continual Learning
